---
format:
  docx:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
---

11 June 2026

Dear Editors of *Ecology Letters*,

Please consider our manuscript, "A flexible modelling framework for estimating thermal tolerance and sensitivity", for publication in *Ecology Letters* as a Methods paper.

Extreme heat is one of the clearest biological consequences of climate change, but ecologists still lack robust tools for translating thermal-tolerance experiments into predictions under realistic exposure. Thermal death time and thermal load sensitivity models are widely used for this purpose, yet the standard analysis remains a two-stage workaround: estimate a time-to-endpoint at each temperature, then regress those estimates against temperature. This workflow is familiar, but it discards uncertainty, struggles with overdispersed and hierarchical data, weakens group comparisons, and can perform poorly under experimental designs already used in the literature.

Our paper provides a direct replacement. We fit a single Bayesian four-parameter logistic model to the raw response data and show how the quantities thermal ecologists already use - thermal sensitivity, critical thermal maxima (CTmax), critical thermal limits (Tcrit), heat injury, and survival - fall out of that one model. Our simulations show that the joint model has far superior statistical properties compared to the two-stage approach currently used. The result keeps the field's biological language while replacing the fragile statistical machinery beneath it. It propagates uncertainty, handles censoring and overdispersion, supports hierarchical designs and group contrasts, and produces posterior predictions under fluctuating temperature regimes (see @fig-cover-framework).

A key contribution we make is accessibility. We provide the `bayesTLS` R package, all data and scripts, and a detailed online supplement and tutorial so readers can reproduce the analyses, apply the workflow to their own data, and extend it to new endpoints or experimental designs.

We believe this manuscript is a strong fit for *Ecology Letters* because it addresses a broad conceptual and practical bottleneck in climate-change ecology: how to move from heat-tolerance experiments to prediction of extreme heat impacts in nature. We show how to fit these new models using the `bayesTLS` R package using empirical data from diverse organisms, including brown shrimp, zebrafish, snow gum, and vinegar flies. We show that the same workflow applies across plants and animals, lethal and sublethal endpoints, counts and continuous proportions, life stages, sexes, random effects, and overdispersed data.

The manuscript is original, is not under consideration elsewhere, and has been approved by all authors.

Thank you for considering our submission.

Sincerely,

Daniel W.A. Noble, Pieter A. Arnold, and Patrice Pottier

![Conceptual overview of the proposed modelling framework.](../output/figs/fig1.png){#fig-cover-framework width=100%}
